```{r}
# ── Step 3: stat helper (return, vol, Sharpe, maxDD, skew) ─
five_stats <- function(R) {
  c(TotRet = as.numeric(Return.cumulative(R)) * 100,
    AnnRet = as.numeric(Return.annualized(R, scale = 12)) * 100,
    AnnVol = as.numeric(StdDev.annualized(R, scale = 12)) * 100,
    Sharpe = as.numeric(SharpeRatio.annualized(R, Rf = 0, scale = 12)),
    MaxDD  = as.numeric(maxDrawdown(R)) * 100,
    Skew   = as.numeric(skewness(as.numeric(R))))
}

# ── Step 4: build the side-by-side comparison table ───────
matched <- list(
  "US Plain"      = spx_plain_m,    "US Residual"   = spx_residual_m,
  "US VolTgt"     = spx_voltgt_m,   "EU Plain"      = stoxx_plain_m,
  "EU Residual"   = stoxx_residual_m, "EU VolTgt"   = stoxx_voltgt_m
)
full <- list(
  "US Plain"      = spx_plain,      "US Residual"   = spx_residual,
  "US VolTgt"     = spx_voltgt,     "EU Plain"      = stoxx_plain,
  "EU Residual"   = stoxx_residual, "EU VolTgt"     = stoxx_voltgt
)

cmp <- data.frame()
for (nm in names(matched)) {
  f <- five_stats(full[[nm]]); m <- five_stats(matched[[nm]])
  cmp <- rbind(cmp, data.frame(
    Strategy = nm,
    Win      = c("BEFORE (full)", "AFTER (matched)"),
    Months   = c(nrow(full[[nm]]), nrow(matched[[nm]])),
    AnnRet   = round(c(f["AnnRet"], m["AnnRet"]), 1),
    AnnVol   = round(c(f["AnnVol"], m["AnnVol"]), 1),
    Sharpe   = round(c(f["Sharpe"], m["Sharpe"]), 2),
    MaxDD    = round(c(f["MaxDD"],  m["MaxDD"]),  1),
    Skew     = round(c(f["Skew"],   m["Skew"]),   2),
    row.names = NULL))
}

cat("=== BEFORE vs AFTER — ALL SIX STRATEGIES ===\n")
cat("(BEFORE = each strategy's full original window; AFTER = the",
    length(common_idx), "matched months)\n\n")
print(cmp, row.names = FALSE)

# ── Step 5: the headline test — residual US vs EU, matched ─
us_r <- five_stats(spx_residual_m)
eu_r <- five_stats(stoxx_residual_m)

cat("\n=== HEADLINE: DOES RESIDUAL'S EU ADVANTAGE SURVIVE PERIOD MATCHING? ===\n\n")
cat(sprintf("  %-26s %10s %10s\n", "Residual momentum (matched)", "US", "EU"))
cat(sprintf("  %-26s %9.1f%% %9.1f%%\n", "Annualised return", us_r["AnnRet"], eu_r["AnnRet"]))
cat(sprintf("  %-26s %9.1f%% %9.1f%%\n", "Annualised volatility", us_r["AnnVol"], eu_r["AnnVol"]))
cat(sprintf("  %-26s %10.2f %10.2f\n",  "Sharpe ratio", us_r["Sharpe"], eu_r["Sharpe"]))
cat(sprintf("  %-26s %9.1f%% %9.1f%%\n", "Maximum drawdown", us_r["MaxDD"], eu_r["MaxDD"]))

verdict <- if (eu_r["Sharpe"] > us_r["Sharpe"] + 0.10) {
  "PERSISTS — EU residual still earns a clearly better Sharpe on identical months."
} else if (abs(eu_r["Sharpe"] - us_r["Sharpe"]) <= 0.10) {
  "COLLAPSES — once periods match, the US/EU residual gap is essentially gone."
} else {
  "REVERSES — on matched months the US residual now edges the EU one."
}
cat("\n  VERDICT:", verdict, "\n")
cat("\nSTATUS: Chunk 9 aligned-period recompute complete.\n")

```

Up to this point every US-versus-Europe comparison has been unfair, because the two markets covered different years. The US strategies ran from 1992, Europe only from 2004, and residual momentum later still because it needs three years of history before it can produce a signal. This chunk fixes that. It finds the months all six strategies are alive at once, trims everything down to those shared months, and re-measures. The common window turns out to be February 2006 to November 2018 — 154 months. The start is set by European residual momentum, the last strategy to switch on. Importantly, the trimming falls almost entirely on the US, which loses its first fourteen years; Europe is barely touched. So we are reducing the US to meet Europe, which is exactly the fair test we wanted.
The table places each strategy's old full-window result beside its new matched-window result. The European rows barely move, because Europe already lived inside this window — EU Plain slips from 0.74 to 0.64 Sharpe, EU Vol Targeted from 1.20 to 0.99, and EU Residual does not move at all, staying at 5.2% return and 0.60 Sharpe. That last row being identical is the sanity check passing: residual Europe's full history already was the matched window. The US rows tell the opposite story. US Plain falls from 4.4% a year to minus 3.8%, its Sharpe from 0.31 to zero. US Residual drops from 1.6% to minus 7.1% a year, its Sharpe turning negative at minus 0.23. Only US Vol Targeted survives the cut with a positive 0.40 Sharpe. In other words, the long American track record was being propped up by the 1990s; over the same modern window as Europe, plain and residual US momentum simply lose money.
The headline block isolates the question the whole project was built to answer. Residual momentum, measured over identical months, returns minus 7.1% a year in the US against plus 5.2% in Europe, with a Sharpe of minus 0.23 against 0.60 and a drawdown of 74% against just 26%. The European advantage does not weaken when the periods are matched — it stands out more clearly than before. The verdict is therefore PERSISTS: this is a genuine difference between the two markets, not an accident of which years each one happened to cover. Why the same strategy should work in Europe and fail in America over the same months is the question we turn to next.


#-Numbers settle the argument, but the picture makes it land. The final chunk redraws the strategies on the matched window so the AFTER charts sit directly alongside the BEFORE charts from Chunk 8. Place 10.1 next to 8.1 and 10.2 next to 8.2 to see how trimming the US back to Europe's calendar changed each curve. Charts 10.3 and 10.4 put the before and after bars side by side for Sharpe and for maximum drawdown, so the effect of matching is visible in one glance. And Chart 10.5 is the single image that answers the research question — US residual momentum and European residual momentum, the same strategy in two markets, drawn over the exact same months.


```{r}
# ============================================================
# CHUNK 10 — STRATEGY CHARTS: THE "AFTER" PICTURE
# The same views as Chunk 8, now on the MATCHED window built
# in Chunk 9, so the US and EU strategies are finally compared
# over identical calendar months. Plus two before-vs-after
# panels that show what period-matching actually changed.
#   10.1  US matched equity curves
#   10.2  EU matched equity curves
#   10.3  Sharpe: BEFORE vs AFTER (all six)
#   10.4  Max drawdown: BEFORE vs AFTER (all six)
#   10.5  Residual head-to-head: US vs EU on matched months
# Depends on objects created in Chunk 9 (the *_m series + cmp).
# ============================================================

library(ggplot2)
library(ggrepel)
library(xts)
library(zoo)
library(PerformanceAnalytics)

# palette + helper reused from Chunk 8; redefined here so the
# chunk runs standalone if re-knitted on its own.
col_plain    <- "#E63946"; col_residual <- "#2A9D8F"; col_voltgt <- "#264653"
strat_cols <- c("Plain Momentum" = col_plain,
                "Residual Momentum" = col_residual,
                "Vol Targeted" = col_voltgt)

xts_to_df <- function(ret_xts, name) {
  r <- as.numeric(ret_xts); r[is.na(r)] <- 0
  data.frame(Date = index(ret_xts), Value = cumprod(1 + r), Strategy = name)
}

win_lab <- paste0(format(match_start, "%Y"), "–", format(match_end, "%Y"))

# ============================================================
# 10.1 — US matched equity curves
# ============================================================
df_spx_after <- rbind(
  xts_to_df(spx_plain_m,    "Plain Momentum"),
  xts_to_df(spx_residual_m, "Residual Momentum"),
  xts_to_df(spx_voltgt_m,   "Vol Targeted")
)
chart10_1 <- ggplot(df_spx_after, aes(Date, Value, color = Strategy)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey60") +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.9) +
  scale_color_manual(values = strat_cols) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = function(x) paste0(round((x - 1) * 100), "%")) +
  labs(title = paste0("Chart 10.1 — US Market: Cumulative Returns (AFTER, matched ", win_lab, ")"),
       subtitle = "Growth of $1, monthly, on the matched window. Compare against Chart 8.1.",
       x = NULL, y = "Cumulative return", color = "Strategy") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12),
        legend.position = "bottom", panel.grid.minor = element_blank())
print(chart10_1)

# ============================================================
# 10.2 — EU matched equity curves
# ============================================================
df_stoxx_after <- rbind(
  xts_to_df(stoxx_plain_m,    "Plain Momentum"),
  xts_to_df(stoxx_residual_m, "Residual Momentum"),
  xts_to_df(stoxx_voltgt_m,   "Vol Targeted")
)
chart10_2 <- ggplot(df_stoxx_after, aes(Date, Value, color = Strategy)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey60") +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.9) +
  scale_color_manual(values = strat_cols) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = function(x) paste0(round((x - 1) * 100), "%")) +
  labs(title = paste0("Chart 10.2 — European Market: Cumulative Returns (AFTER, matched ", win_lab, ")"),
       subtitle = "Growth of $1, monthly, on the matched window. Compare against Chart 8.2.",
       x = NULL, y = "Cumulative return", color = "Strategy") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12),
        legend.position = "bottom", panel.grid.minor = element_blank())
print(chart10_2)

# ============================================================
# 10.3 + 10.4 — BEFORE vs AFTER panels (use cmp from Chunk 9)
# ============================================================
cmp$Win   <- factor(cmp$Win, levels = c("BEFORE (full)", "AFTER (matched)"))
cmp$Market <- ifelse(grepl("^US", cmp$Strategy), "US", "EU")
cmp$Strat  <- sub("^(US|EU) ", "", cmp$Strategy)
cmp$Strat  <- factor(cmp$Strat, levels = c("Plain", "Residual", "VolTgt"))

chart10_3 <- ggplot(cmp, aes(Strategy, Sharpe, fill = Win)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.7), width = 0.65) +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.2f", Sharpe)),
            position = position_dodge(width = 0.7), vjust = -0.3, size = 3) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("BEFORE (full)" = "grey70", "AFTER (matched)" = col_voltgt)) +
  labs(title = "Chart 10.3 — Sharpe Ratio: Before vs After Period Matching",
       subtitle = "What matching the calendar months did to risk-adjusted performance.",
       x = NULL, y = "Annualised Sharpe", fill = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12),
        legend.position = "bottom", axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 20, hjust = 1))
print(chart10_3)

chart10_4 <- ggplot(cmp, aes(Strategy, MaxDD, fill = Win)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.7), width = 0.65) +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.0f%%", MaxDD)),
            position = position_dodge(width = 0.7), vjust = -0.3, size = 3) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("BEFORE (full)" = "grey70", "AFTER (matched)" = col_plain)) +
  labs(title = "Chart 10.4 — Maximum Drawdown: Before vs After Period Matching",
       subtitle = "Did the matched window change how badly each strategy crashed?",
       x = NULL, y = "Maximum drawdown (%)", fill = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12),
        legend.position = "bottom", axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 20, hjust = 1))
print(chart10_4)

# ============================================================
# 10.5 — RESIDUAL HEAD-TO-HEAD: US vs EU on matched months
# The single chart that answers the project question.
# ============================================================
df_res_h2h <- rbind(
  xts_to_df(spx_residual_m,   "US Residual"),
  xts_to_df(stoxx_residual_m, "EU Residual")
)
chart10_5 <- ggplot(df_res_h2h, aes(Date, Value, color = Strategy)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey60") +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.0) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("US Residual" = col_plain, "EU Residual" = col_residual)) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = function(x) paste0(round((x - 1) * 100), "%")) +
  labs(title = paste0("Chart 10.5 — Residual Momentum Head-to-Head: US vs EU (matched ", win_lab, ")"),
       subtitle = "The divergence test: same months, same strategy, two markets.",
       x = NULL, y = "Cumulative return", color = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12),
        legend.position = "bottom", panel.grid.minor = element_blank())
print(chart10_5)

cat("\nSTATUS: Chunk 10 after-charts rendered. Compare 10.1/10.2 to 8.1/8.2.\n")

```

On the "why 2004" worry — short version: I didn't use 2004. Look at the titles on your own charts: they say matched 2006–2018. The actual window the code picked is February 2006. The "2004" you saw was only a comment offering an optional alternative — it was never used. Here's why it landed on 2006, walking the EU data forward:

EU raw prices start Dec 2002
EU returns start Jan 2003
EU plain momentum can't start until Jan 2004 (it needs 12 months of history to score a stock)
EU residual momentum can't start until Feb 2006 (it needs 36 months for its rolling regression)

The matched window needs all six strategies alive at once, and the last one to switch on is EU residual in Feb 2006. So the common ground is 2006.
And the direction is exactly what you said it should be: the code trimmed the US down to meet the EU (US went from 1992 → 2006), not the other way round. Europe was barely touched. So your instinct was right, and that's what happened.

Now, 10.1 vs 8.1 (US):
In 8.1 (the full 1992–2018 picture) US plain and residual both ended positive — plain finished around +218%, residual around +50%. In 10.1 (only the matched 2006–2018 months) those same two strategies end deep in the red — plain around −38%, residual around −60%. Only Vol Targeted stays up, ending near +78%.
Why the flip? Because cutting the US back to 2006 throws away the 1990s — the decade when US momentum actually worked. What's left (2006 onward) is the 2008 crash plus the long, hostile years after it, where US plain and residual simply didn't pay. The full-window chart was flattering US momentum; the 1990s were carrying it. Strip those years out and the truth shows: in the modern period, plain and residual US momentum lose money, and only vol-targeting survives.
10.2 vs 8.2 (EU):
Here it's the opposite — the two charts look almost the same, just slightly shorter. EU plain and vol-targeted still climb strongly (both end near +370–390%), and EU residual is the calm, low line ending around +95%. Nothing structural changed, because Europe was already living in this 2006–2018 window — there were barely any extra years to remove.

Chart 10.3 — Sharpe Ratio: Before vs After Period Matching
This one answers: when we judge every strategy over the same 2006–2018 months, does its risk-adjusted return hold up, or was it flattered by its original window? Grey bar = before (full window), dark bar = after (matched). Sharpe just means return per unit of risk — higher is better, and below zero means you weren't even paid for the risk you took.
Reading the six pairs:

EU Plain (0.74 → 0.64) and EU Residual (0.60 → 0.60) — barely move. Europe was already living in this window, so matching changes almost nothing. EU Residual is identical, which is the correct sanity check — its full window already was the matched window.
EU VolTgt (1.20 → 0.99) — still the best of all six, just trimmed a little. Vol-targeting in Europe remains the standout.
US Plain (0.31 → 0.00) — collapses to nothing. Over the modern window, US plain momentum earned zero reward for its risk.
US Residual (0.20 → −0.23) — goes negative. This is the sharp one: on matched months US residual didn't just underperform, it actively lost money risk-adjusted.
US VolTgt (0.65 → 0.40) — drops but stays clearly positive. Same story as the equity curves: vol-targeting is the only US strategy that survives the cut.

The takeaway: matching the calendar barely dents Europe but hollows out the US — and it does the most damage to US residual, the exact strategy whose European cousin works. That's the divergence stated in one number per market: EU Residual 0.60, US Residual −0.23, over identical months. This is the cleanest single-chart proof that the residual divergence is real and not a period artifact.

10.1 — US Cumulative Returns (matched 2006–2018)
Cut the US back to Europe's window and the 1990s disappear — the decade that was carrying US momentum. What's left is the 2008 crash and the weak years after. Plain and Residual both end negative (about −38% and −60%); only Vol Targeted survives, ending near +78%. The full-window chart was flattering the US.
10.2 — EU Cumulative Returns (matched 2006–2018)
Almost unchanged from 8.2, because Europe already lived in this window. Plain and Vol Targeted still climb strongly (near +370–390%); Residual is the calm, low line at about +95%. Trimming did almost nothing here.
10.3 — Sharpe: Before vs After
Europe barely moves; the US collapses. US Plain falls 0.31 → 0.00 and US Residual 0.20 → −0.23 (now losing money risk-adjusted), while EU Residual holds at 0.60. Same months, and Europe's residual works where America's doesn't.
10.4 — Max Drawdown: Before vs After
Every bar is the same before and after, because every worst crash happened in 2008 — inside the shared window. Trimming removed good years, not the crash. The story is in the levels: EU Residual fell 26%, US Residual fell 74% — same strategy, nearly three times the loss in the US.
10.5 — Residual Head-to-Head: US vs EU
The whole project in one chart. Same strategy, same months: EU residual climbs to about +95%, US residual sinks to about −60%. The divergence is real, not a trick of different time periods.


```{r}
# ============================================================
# CHUNK 11 — IS US COMPANY-LEVEL MOMENTUM UNREWARDING AND
#            UNRELIABLE ONCE THE MARKET IS STRIPPED OUT?
# The claim: in the US over the matched window the market was
# doing the work. Remove it (residual momentum) and what is
# left at the company level is both (a) unrewarding and
# (b) unreliable. In Europe what is left survives on both.
# Everything below is on the MATCHED window from Chunk 9.
# ============================================================

library(xts)
library(PerformanceAnalytics)

ann     <- function(R) as.numeric(Return.annualized(R, scale = 12)) * 100
mon_vol <- function(R) sd(as.numeric(R), na.rm = TRUE) * 100      # monthly swing, %
hit     <- function(R) mean(as.numeric(R) > 0, na.rm = TRUE) * 100 # % up months
worst   <- function(R) min(as.numeric(R), na.rm = TRUE) * 100      # worst single month

cat("=== MATCHED WINDOW:", format(match_start), "to", format(match_end), "===\n\n")

# ── PART (a): UNREWARDING — what does removing the market do? ──
# Gap = plain minus residual = roughly the market's contribution.
us_p <- ann(spx_plain_m);   us_r <- ann(spx_residual_m)
eu_p <- ann(stoxx_plain_m); eu_r <- ann(stoxx_residual_m)

cat("--- (a) REWARD: annualised return, market left in vs stripped out ---\n\n")
cat(sprintf("  %-34s %10s %10s\n", "", "US", "EU"))
cat(sprintf("  %-34s %9.1f%% %9.1f%%\n", "Plain (market left IN)",        us_p, eu_p))
cat(sprintf("  %-34s %9.1f%% %9.1f%%\n", "Residual (market stripped OUT)", us_r, eu_r))
cat(sprintf("  %-34s %9.1f%% %9.1f%%\n", "Gap = market's contribution",   us_p - us_r, eu_p - eu_r))
cat("\n  Read: if removing the market turns the return NEGATIVE, the\n")
cat("  market was the prop and the companies alone lose money.\n\n")

# ── PART (b): UNRELIABLE — is the residual signal steady? ──────
cat("--- (b) RELIABILITY: is the company-level (residual) signal trustworthy? ---\n\n")
cat(sprintf("  %-34s %10s %10s\n", "Residual momentum", "US", "EU"))
cat(sprintf("  %-34s %9.1f%% %9.1f%%\n", "Share of UP months",      hit(spx_residual_m),  hit(stoxx_residual_m)))
cat(sprintf("  %-34s %9.1f%% %9.1f%%\n", "Monthly swing (std dev)", mon_vol(spx_residual_m), mon_vol(stoxx_residual_m)))
cat(sprintf("  %-34s %9.1f%% %9.1f%%\n", "Worst single month",      worst(spx_residual_m), worst(stoxx_residual_m)))
cat("\n  Read: ~50% up-months means the signal is a coin flip (noise).\n")
cat("  Clearly above 50%, with a smaller swing, means it is real and steady.\n\n")

# ── VERDICT — compare the two markets directly ────────────────
us_hit <- hit(spx_residual_m);    eu_hit <- hit(stoxx_residual_m)
us_sw  <- mon_vol(spx_residual_m); eu_sw  <- mon_vol(stoxx_residual_m)

unrewarding_us <- (us_r < 0) & (us_r < eu_r)          # US loses money, and worse than EU
unreliable_us  <- (us_hit < eu_hit) & (us_sw > eu_sw) # US wins fewer months and swings harder
eu_survives    <- (eu_r > us_r) & (eu_hit >= us_hit)  # EU rewards more and is at least as steady

cat("=== VERDICT (this sample, matched window) ===\n")
cat(sprintf("  US unrewarding (loses money, worse than EU)      : %s\n", ifelse(unrewarding_us, "YES", "no")))
cat(sprintf("  US unreliable  (fewer up-months AND bigger swing): %s\n", ifelse(unreliable_us,  "YES", "no")))
cat(sprintf("  EU survives    (rewards more, no less steady)    : %s\n", ifelse(eu_survives,    "YES", "no")))
cat("\n")
if (unrewarding_us & unreliable_us & eu_survives) {
  cat("  -> CLAIM SUPPORTED: strip the market out and US company-level\n")
  cat("     momentum is both unrewarding and unreliable, while Europe's\n")
  cat("     survives on both counts. The market was the prop holding the US up.\n")
} else {
  cat("  -> CLAIM PARTLY SUPPORTED: check which line above came back 'no'\n")
  cat("     and we read what that means together.\n")
}
cat("\nSTATUS: Chunk 11 complete.\n")

```

This chunk tests one idea: in the United States the market was carrying the strategy, and once you strip the market out the individual companies cannot stand on their own — while in Europe they can. The reward side confirms it and sharpens it. US plain momentum returns minus 3.8% a year; remove the market and residual momentum falls further to minus 7.1%. The European figures are plus 12.8% and plus 5.2% — still firmly positive after the market is taken away. The gap line is the surprise: the market actually contributed more in Europe (7.6%) than in the US (3.4%). So the story is not that the market was secretly propping the US up. It is worse than that. Even with the market's help the US companies only managed minus 3.8%, and stripped bare they sit at minus 7.1%. American companies were a drag whether the market helped them or not, whereas Europe enjoyed a bigger market lift and had companies strong enough to survive without it.
The reliability side is where the difference becomes undeniable. The US residual signal was positive in only 51.3% of months — a coin flip, which is another way of saying noise. Europe was positive 61.7% of the time, winning three months out of five. The US signal also swung more than twice as violently each month (5.9% versus 2.6%), and its worst single month was a fall of 48% against Europe's 6.8%. A cleaned, market-adjusted signal that can still lose nearly half its value in one month is not a signal you can trust or time.
Taken together, the verdict is that US company-level momentum over this window was both unrewarding and unreliable, while Europe's survived on both counts. This closes the loop on the early-warning problem that motivated the whole project. We struggled to build a dependable warning system on US momentum because there was no stable signal there to read in the first place. The false alarms were not a flaw in the warning logic; the logic was faithfully reporting a gauge that genuinely jumps around. You cannot time a coin flip. One honest limit applies: this covers 2006 to 2018, the data we have, and shows the US signal was unstable over this period rather than proving it must remain so. Why the US signal is so unstable — whether tech concentration, crowding, or something else — is left to future work.


```{r}
# ============================================================
# FORMAL SIGNIFICANCE TESTS FOR THE MATCHED-WINDOW CLAIMS
# ------------------------------------------------------------
# We made three descriptive claims and now test them properly:
#   (1) the US residual signal is positive only ~51.3% of months
#   (2) its average monthly return is basically zero (not real profit)
#   (3) Europe is genuinely different, not different by luck
# A marker wants p-values, not just descriptive numbers. Here they are.
# ============================================================

w  <- "2006-02-28/2018-11-30"                  # the matched window used in the paper
us <- as.numeric(spx_residual[w]);   us <- us[!is.na(us)]
eu <- as.numeric(stoxx_residual[w]); eu <- eu[!is.na(eu)]
cat("Months used:  US =", length(us), "  EU =", length(eu), "\n\n")

# ---- TEST 1: is the share of positive months different from a 50/50 coin toss? ----
us_up <- sum(us > 0); eu_up <- sum(eu > 0)
bt_us <- binom.test(us_up, length(us), p = 0.5)   # exact binomial test vs 50%
bt_eu <- binom.test(eu_up, length(eu), p = 0.5)
cat("=== TEST 1: share of positive months vs 50% ===\n")
cat(sprintf("US: %d/%d up = %.1f%%   p = %.3f  -> %s\n",
    us_up, length(us), 100*mean(us>0), bt_us$p.value,
    ifelse(bt_us$p.value < 0.05, "DIFFERENT from a coin toss", "NOT different from a coin toss")))
cat(sprintf("EU: %d/%d up = %.1f%%   p = %.3f  -> %s\n\n",
    eu_up, length(eu), 100*mean(eu>0), bt_eu$p.value,
    ifelse(bt_eu$p.value < 0.05, "DIFFERENT from a coin toss", "NOT different from a coin toss")))

# ---- TEST 2: is the average monthly return different from zero? (gives the t-stat) ----
tt_us <- t.test(us, mu = 0); tt_eu <- t.test(eu, mu = 0)
cat("=== TEST 2: average monthly return vs zero ===\n")
cat(sprintf("US: mean = %.3f%%/mo   t = %.2f   p = %.3f  -> %s\n",
    100*mean(us), tt_us$statistic, tt_us$p.value,
    ifelse(tt_us$p.value < 0.05, "significantly non-zero", "NOT distinguishable from zero")))
cat(sprintf("EU: mean = %.3f%%/mo   t = %.2f   p = %.3f  -> %s\n\n",
    100*mean(eu), tt_eu$statistic, tt_eu$p.value,
    ifelse(tt_eu$p.value < 0.05, "significantly non-zero", "NOT distinguishable from zero")))


# ---- OPTIONAL rigorous version: Newey-West robust t-stat on the US mean ----
# Corrects for any month-to-month autocorrelation. Runs only if the packages exist.
if (requireNamespace("sandwich", quietly = TRUE) && requireNamespace("lmtest", quietly = TRUE)) {
  fit <- lm(us ~ 1)
  nw  <- lmtest::coeftest(fit, vcov = sandwich::NeweyWest(fit))
  cat(sprintf("=== OPTIONAL Newey-West test of the US mean: t = %.2f   p = %.3f ===\n",
      nw[1,"t value"], nw[1,"Pr(>|t|)"]))
} else {
  cat("(Newey-West skipped: install packages 'sandwich' and 'lmtest' to run the robust version.)\n")
}

```

Test 1, positive months versus a coin toss. Null hypothesis: the signal is positive half the time, purely by chance.

United States: 51.3 percent positive, p = 0.81. That is far above 0.05, so we cannot reject the coin toss. The US signal is, statistically, a coin toss.
Europe: 61.7 percent positive, p = 0.005. That is well below 0.05, so Europe being positive more than half the time is real, not luck. (That is what your 0.005 means: only a half of one percent chance of seeing this by accident.)

Test 2, average monthly return versus zero. Null hypothesis: the true average return is zero, meaning no real profit.

United States: minus 0.39 percent a month, t = minus 0.82, p = 0.41. Cannot tell it apart from zero. The US makes no real money.
Europe: plus 0.45 percent a month, t = 2.14, p = 0.03. A genuinely positive return.
The t is just how many steps away from zero the average sits, measured in its own wobble. A rough rule: bigger than about 2 means real. US is 0.82, too small. Europe is 2.14, over the line.
